directions.name: Retune the accepted B-first cp.async handoff inside the K16 hot band
directions.hypothesis: The accepted round-52/53 base is still the right surface: grouped-row=8, K16, no B lookahead, single-scratch export, and B-first refill ordering. The latest run only regressed by 0.009152 ms, so the remaining gap looks like a very narrow steady-state handoff issue between cp.async issue, wait/commit timing, and the first PTX B-fragment consume order rather than a new memory-balance problem. Keep the accepted base intact and tighten only the B-first feed/issue handoff.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Async-copy feed/issue retiming and stage handoff latency inside the active PTX hot-band microkernel.
directions.risk: Low to moderate. The upside is the best in this round, but it is easy to slide back into a disguised unroll-1 or lookahead-style rewrite if the handoff is changed too broadly.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime versus 24.895488 ms accepted base
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the change does not beat the 24.895488 ms accepted base, or if it starts behaving like another K32 or unroll-1 branch.

directions.name: Trim PTX export and accumulator live range on the accepted single-scratch surface
directions.hypothesis: Round 53 kept the accepted base intact but still landed slightly slower than round 52, while tensor activity stayed only mid-48% and active warps stayed low. That makes register pressure and export-side live range the best deferred cleanup family: shorten operand or accumulator residency, tighten the single-scratch export handoff, and recover issue capacity without reopening the disproved lookahead or stage branches.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register pressure and export-side synchronization / live-range overhead in the PTX hot-band epilogue.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is narrower than a stage rewrite, but it can still raise register count or add synchronization if the lifetime trim is not truly local.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if register count or occupancy pressure worsens without a clear runtime win over the accepted base.

directions.name: Reopen the warmup A-first / refill B-first branch only as a closure probe
directions.hypothesis: The latest measured experiment already tested the A-first warmup / B-first refill idea and regressed slightly versus the accepted base. With DRAM still low and the stall mix pointing at steady-state handoff timing instead of raw memory latency, this branch is not the next best move; it only remains as a closure probe to confirm the branch stays closed unless new evidence appears.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warmup ordering is not the primary limiter; the current loss is in steady-state handoff timing on the accepted base.
directions.risk: High. This reopens a measured-negative branch and is unlikely to outperform the accepted B-first surface without new evidence.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime versus 24.895488 ms accepted base
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if it does not beat the accepted base or if it reintroduces the same regression pattern from the failed warmup-order experiment.

notes: Accepted this round: the B-first cp.async handoff family, constrained to the already-accepted grouped-row=8 + K16 + no-lookahead + single-scratch export base. Deferred: export/live-range trimming, because it is still plausible but secondary to the handoff retiming. Rejected: reopening the A-first warmup / B-first refill branch, because the latest experiment regressed and the evidence still points to steady-state handoff timing rather than warmup order.
